title: Injera

overview.description: A traditional Ethiopian fermented flatbread made from teff flour. Soft, slightly spongy and pleasantly tangy, injera is both the plate and the utensil for many Ethiopian meals.

method: Mix the teff flour and warm water into a smooth batter. Cover loosely and leave at room temperature to ferment for 48–72 hours. The batter should become bubbly and develop a pleasant sour aroma.
Stir the batter gently and add the salt.
Heat a large non-stick frying pan over a medium heat. Do not add any oil.
Pour enough batter into the pan to cover the base in a thin, even layer, swirling the pan if necessary.
Cover immediately with a lid and cook for about 2–3 minutes. The surface should become covered in tiny holes while the bottom cooks.
Do not flip the injera. It is cooked only on one side.
Carefully remove and leave to cool completely before stacking. Repeat with the remaining batter.

learning.did-you-know: Injera has been made in Ethiopia for centuries. Traditionally it is served underneath the food and used instead of cutlery to scoop up stews.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How natural fermentation develops flavour, how batter breads differ from dough breads, and how to recognise when fermentation is complete.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: An authentic bread that opens the door to Ethiopian cooking. Although it requires patience, it's one of the most distinctive breads in the collection.
chapman-tips: Temperature greatly affects fermentation time—warmer kitchens ferment faster.
The batter should smell pleasantly sour, never unpleasant.
A lid is essential to cook the top without flipping the bread.
notes.batch-cooking: Once cooled, stack between sheets of baking paper and freeze for up to three months.
notes.leftovers: Excellent reheated briefly in a covered pan or steamer.
